aids.title: Spanning Tree as Road Network
aids.content: Imagine a city with many roads between neighborhoods. A spanning tree is like keeping just enough roads so every neighborhood is reachable, with no redundant routes. You need exactly (neighborhoods - 1) roads. Cut any one road, and some neighborhood gets disconnected.
aids.forRemembering: What a spanning tree represents and why it has V-1 edges

aids.title: n-1 Rule
aids.content: A tree is 'one short' of its vertex count: n vertices, n-1 edges. Think 'Trees are modest -- they always have one less edge than vertices.'
aids.forRemembering: The edge count property of spanning trees

aids.title: Add Edge = One Cycle, Remove Edge = Two Parts
aids.content: Two key facts for exams: (1) Adding any non-tree edge to a spanning tree creates exactly one cycle. (2) Removing any edge from a spanning tree splits it into exactly two components. These are frequently tested.
aids.forRemembering: Effects of adding/removing edges in spanning trees

aids.title: BFS Tree = Shortest Paths
aids.content: The BFS spanning tree is also called the shortest-path tree because the path from the root to any vertex in this tree has the minimum number of edges. The DFS tree does NOT have this property.
aids.forRemembering: Distinguishing BFS spanning tree from DFS spanning tree
